title: Root-mode history compression is lossy-on-lossy — per-message 400-char clip + 300-token global summary
what_failed: When root history reaches 30+ messages, compress_messages_with_groq summarizes everything older than keep_recent=12 into a single bullet block. The information is destroyed twice: (1) _build_context clips each source message to 400 chars before the summarizer ever sees it, and (2) the summarizer is capped at max_tokens=300 and prompted for '3-5 bullet points' regardless of how many messages it is compressing. An 18-message stretch of dense coding/reasoning collapses to ~5 bullets of ~60 words.
where_failed: agent/truncation.py:45 (content[:400] in _build_context), agent/truncation.py:32-35 (_COMPRESS_PROMPT '3-5 bullet points'), agent/truncation.py:93 (max_tokens=300)
why_likely: Compression was built to keep Claude token cost down (T-092). The caps were chosen for cost, not for how much context is actually needed to continue coherently. No measurement of post-compression coherence was done.
migration_plan: 1. Raise the per-message clip in _build_context from 400 to ~1200 chars (or make it proportional to how many messages are being compressed).
2. Scale summary budget with input size: max_tokens = min(1024, 60 * n_messages_compressed) instead of a flat 300.
3. Reword _COMPRESS_PROMPT to 'preserve all decisions, file paths, identifiers, numbers, and open questions verbatim' rather than '3-5 bullets'.
4. Consider a rolling-summary approach (summary of summary + recent) instead of one-shot, so old context degrades gracefully instead of falling off a cliff at message 30.
5. Add a test: compress a known 20-message conversation, assert specific facts (a file path, a number) survive into the summary.
implementation_notes: Lower priority than T-149 because root mode at least keeps 12 recent messages verbatim and sends the real message array. The compression only bites on long sessions. But for a system meant to do multi-hour engineering work, long sessions are the point.
analysis: PERSPECTIVE: Cost-driven caps are fine until they silently degrade the product. 300 tokens to summarize an entire engineering session is absurdly tight — that's cheaper than the bug it causes. Scale the budget with the input. The rolling-summary idea (step 4) is the right long-term shape; one-shot compression at a hard threshold is a cliff, and cliffs are where coherence dies.
